did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used three web-based multimodal large language models (ChatGPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude Sonnet 4) to perform BCC detection and subtype classification from clinical and dermoscopic images. The AI models were evaluated for their diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity in distinguishing basal cell carcinoma from mimickers.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a comparative performance analysis involving original experimental data. The study evaluated 772 clinical and dermoscopic images using three specific web-based multimodal large language models (ChatGPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude Sonnet 4) to assess diagnostic accuracy for basal cell carcinoma. It includes a Methods section describing the dataset (290 histopathology-confirmed BCCs and 370 mimickers), standardized prompts, and statistical analysis of primary and secondary outcomes, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude in clinical and dermoscopic image analysis of basal cell carcinoma and its common mimickers: A comparative performance analysis
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of three off-the-shelf multimodal large language models—ChatGPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude Sonnet 4—for basal cell carcinoma (BCC) detection and subtype classification using clinical and dermoscopic images. The dataset comprised 772 images from 290 histopathology-confirmed BCC cases and 370 BCC mimickers. The primary outcome was BCC detection accuracy, while secondary outcomes included subtype classification accuracy and performance stratified by lesion features. For clinical images, ChatGPT-5 achieved the highest detection accuracy (75%), whereas Claude Sonnet 4 performed best for dermoscopic images (69.8%). Subtype classification accuracy was modest across all models, and performance varied significantly by lesion characteristics such as pigmentation and growth pattern. The authors concluded that current web-based LLMs are not clinically suitable for BCC detection or subtyping due to insufficient accuracy and high false-positive rates.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to distinguish basal cell carcinoma from BCC mimickers using clinical and dermoscopic images. Evaluated via sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy.

Multiclass classification task to identify specific histopathological subtypes of BCC (e.g., nodular, superficial, infiltrative, micronodular, morpheaform, basosquamous, mixed) from clinical and dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: Histopathology-confirmed Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) Cohort
datasets_used.description: A cohort of 290 histopathology-confirmed BCC cases collected from specialized European centers. The cohort included 290 clinical images and 112 dermoscopic images. Patient demographics were predominantly fair-skinned (Fitzpatrick types I-III).

use.testing: Used as the positive class for BCC detection and as the ground truth for BCC subtype classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Independent BCC-Mimicker Cohort
datasets_used.description: An independent cohort of 370 non-BCC skin lesions serving as mimickers, collected from specialized European centers. The cohort included 250 clinical images and 120 dermoscopic images.

use.testing: Used as the negative class (non-BCC) for BCC detection tasks.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess statistically significant differences in recall (sensitivity) stratified by lesion characteristics such as crusting, growth pattern, ulceration, and pigmentation.
To estimate the uncertainty of diagnostic performance metrics including sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), likelihood ratios, and accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in recall (sensitivity) for correctly identifying BCCs between the evaluated LLMs, or between lesion subgroups (e.g., crusted vs. noncrusted, exophytic vs. flat, ulcerated vs. nonulcerated, pigmented vs. nonpigmented).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in recall (sensitivity) for correctly identifying BCCs between the evaluated LLMs or between lesion subgroups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistically significant differences in recall were observed when stratified by crusting (P < .0001) and growth pattern (P < .002), with all models performing worse in crusted lesions and better in exophytic lesions. No statistically significant differences were observed for ulceration (P = .9) or pigmentation (P = .2). For dermoscopic images, no statistically significant differences in recall were observed among the LLMs (P = .1).

Clinical Images (BCC Detection).description: Diagnostic performance for BCC detection (BCC vs non-BCC) using clinical images from 290 histopathology-confirmed BCC cases and 370 BCC-mimicker lesions.
Clinical Images (BCC Detection).notes: Statistically significant difference in recall among models (chi-square = 96.46, P < .0001). Performance varied by lesion characteristics: crusted lesions had lower recall (P < .0001); exophytic lesions had higher recall (P < .002). No significant difference by ulceration (P = .9) or pigmentation (P = .2).

Dermoscopic Images (BCC Detection).description: Diagnostic performance for BCC detection (BCC vs non-BCC) using dermoscopic images from 112 BCC cases and 120 BCC-mimicker lesions.
Dermoscopic Images (BCC Detection).notes: No statistically significant difference in recall among models (chi-square = 3.451, P = .1).

Clinical Images (BCC Subtyping).description: Diagnostic performance for BCC subtype classification using clinical images. Metrics represent sensitivity for the specific subtype, specificity for non-that-subtype, PPV, NPV, and accuracy for that subtype.
Clinical Images (BCC Subtyping).notes: Subtype classification accuracy was modest across models. ChatGPT-5 performed best on mnBCC (71.4%) and nBCC (70.9%). Claude Sonnet 4 peaked on mixed subtype (61.3%). Gemini 2.5 Flash peaked on mnBCC (28.6%).

Dermoscopic Images (BCC Subtyping).description: Diagnostic performance for BCC subtype classification using dermoscopic images.
Dermoscopic Images (BCC Subtyping).notes: Overall performance for subtyping was highest for Claude Sonnet 4 (55.4%), followed by Gemini (33%) and ChatGPT (30.4%).

architecture_used.main_model: Three web-based multimodal large language models: ChatGPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude Sonnet 4.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported; models were used as off-the-shelf, open-access web-based services.
architecture_used.input_size: Not reported; images were uploaded via web interface.

training_details.details: No training was performed; the study evaluated pre-existing, commercially available models.

classification_or_output_head.details: Models provided diagnostic labels (BCC vs non-BCC) and subtype classifications based on standardized prompts.

preprocessing_techniques: Standardized prompts were used for image analysis (no specific image preprocessing techniques such as resizing, normalization, or artifact removal were reported; images were uploaded via web interface).
conclusions: For clinical images, ChatGPT-5 achieved the highest BCC detection accuracy (75%), followed by Claude Sonnet 4 (64.3%) and Gemini 2.5 Flash (50.7%).
For dermoscopic images, Claude Sonnet 4 performed best with an accuracy of 69.8%, compared with ChatGPT-5 (55.2%) and Gemini 2.5 Flash (50.9%).
BCC detection accuracy was lower in crusted and flat lesions and higher in exophytic lesions.
The effect of pigmentation on detection performance was model-dependent.
Subtype-classification accuracy was modest across all evaluated models.
Current web-based large language models are not clinically suitable for BCC detection or subtyping.
Dermatology-specific training, transparent reporting, and rigorous prospective validation are required before any clinical use of these models.
The study had limitations including images primarily from European centers with limited skin-type diversity and several small subgroups.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a dataset derived from specialized European centers with predominantly fair-skinned patients (Fitzpatrick types I-III), raising concerns about generalizability to darker skin phototypes and primary care settings.

Several BCC subtypes, particularly superficial BCC (sBCC) and micronodular BCC (mnBCC), were underrepresented in the cohort, limiting the statistical power and stability of subtype classification estimates.

The study utilized high-quality images taken by trained medical personnel in hospital settings, which may not reflect the variable lighting, focus, and quality of real-world clinical or patient-acquired images.
The evaluation relied solely on clinical or dermoscopic images, ignoring other critical diagnostic inputs used in practice such as patient age, sex, lesion location, and palpation findings.
No details regarding the training data, population balance, or metadata of the commercial LLMs were available, making it difficult to fully interpret potential biases in model performance.
The study design was retrospective and experimental; rigorous prospective validation in clinician-in-the-loop settings is required before any clinical deployment.
Standardized prompts were used to simulate user interaction without dermatological knowledge, which may not capture the full capabilities of the models when provided with detailed clinical context.
The authors mitigated conversation-history bias by reinitializing chat sessions every 5 lesions, a conservative approach to prevent pattern priming effects observed in pilot testing.
